perturbation.original_text: Each Party receiving Confidential Information (as defined below) (each, a "Recipient," and together, the "Recipients"), is receiving confidential disclosure of certain confidential or proprietary technical, business, operational, financial and/or commercial information, trade secret information and/or any information of the other designated as “Confidential”, “Proprietary” or by similar designation, or which under the circumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential ("Confidential Information") for the purpose of exploring, establishing, performing or carrying out a contractual business relationship for the production by the Producer of the Commercial and/or other commercial productions for the Client (collectively and severally, the “Transaction").
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party receiving Confidential Information (as defined below) (each, a "Recipient," and together, the "Recipients"), is receiving confidential disclosure of certain information, regardless of whether it's marked as confidential or not (hereafter known as “Confidential Information”) for the purpose of exploring, establishing, performing or carrying out any kind of relationship between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: This change broadens the scope of 'Confidential Information' by stating it includes *any* information received, irrespective of its designation or the nature of the relationship, contradicting the original definition that limited it to designated confidential information relevant to a specific contractual business relationship. This is an in-text contradiction because paragraph 1 says that the information need to be of the other designated as “Confidential”, “Proprietary” or by similar designation. This contradiction raises uncertainty about what information is actually protected.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party receiving Confidential Information (as defined below) (each, a "Recipient," and together, the "Recipients"), is receiving confidential disclosure of certain information, regardless of whether it's marked as confidential or not (hereafter known as “Confidential Information”) for the purpose of exploring, establishing, performing or carrying out any kind of relationship between the parties. Each disclosing Party (each a "Discloser") would not disclose its Confidential Information without the Recipient's agreement to maintain the Confidential Information in confidence and to use such Confidential Information solely as permitted under this Agreement. Confidential Information can be in any form (e.g. electronic, digital, analog, hard copy or oral). Nothing in this Agreement shall require a Party to disclose its Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include *any* information, regardless of its designation. This directly contradicts the original implicit requirement that Confidential Information must be 'designated as Confidential, Proprietary or by similar designation.' The contradicted text mentions that the parties would not disclose Confidential information without the Recipient's agreement, but the change removes the specific requirement, therefore creating a contradiction. This is a strong contradiction that would likely cause confusion about what information is protected under the agreement, based on the explanation given.

perturbation.original_text: Recipients will not disclose, distribute, copy or use any Confidential Information or the fact that a Transaction is being considered or is under a production contract without the prior written consent of the other Party and then only to the extent specified in such consent. Confidential Information may be used only to the extent reasonably required for the purposes of evaluating, producing or carrying out the Transaction; provided however, that Recipients may only disclose the Confidential Information to those of its contractors, vendors, directors, officers, advisors, agents and employees ("Representatives") who need to know such Confidential Information for the purpose of evaluating, performing or carrying out or producing the Transaction and who shall be bound by a written agreement or other legally enforceable written obligations to preserve the confidentiality of the Confidential Information in a manner consistent with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipients will not disclose, distribute, copy or use any Confidential Information or the fact that a Transaction is being considered or is under a production contract without the prior written consent of the other Party, except when required to do so by a legal process. Confidential Information may be used only to the extent reasonably required for the purposes of evaluating, producing or carrying out the Transaction; provided however, that Recipients may disclose the Confidential Information to any of its Representatives, without any requirement for a confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces two in-text contradictions: First, it adds an exception allowing disclosure of Confidential Information when required by legal process, without specifying the type of legal process or any safeguards. Second, it removes the requirement for Representatives to be bound by a confidentiality agreement. This contradicts the original text's emphasis on safeguarding confidential information and creates uncertainty about the actual level of protection for the Discloser’s confidential information. It goes agains the second to last sentence of the same paragraph, where the original texts emphasizes the representatives, need to have an obligation to preserve confidentiality of the Confidential Information
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipients will not disclose, distribute, copy or use any Confidential Information or the fact that a Transaction is being considered or is under a production contract without the prior written consent of the other Party, except when required to do so by a legal process. Confidential Information may be used only to the extent reasonably required for the purposes of evaluating, producing or carrying out the Transaction; provided however, that Recipients may disclose the Confidential Information to any of its Representatives, without any requirement for a confidentiality agreement. The Recipient will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by it or its Representatives, whether or not such Representatives are associated with the Discloser at the time of the breach. Without limiting the foregoing, where the Discloser is the Producer and a Recipient Representative is a cost consultant of the Client or Agency, the Recipient shall secure its Representative’s express written agreement that it will not make any business or other use of, and will in perpetuity hold in confidence, all Confidential Information acquired or learned by such Representative and shall not use or disclose such information except as permitted in this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an in-text contradiction by removing the requirement for Representatives to be bound by a confidentiality agreement. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which makes the recipient responsible for any breach of the agreement by its representatives, indicating an expectation that representatives are bound by confidentiality. Furthermore, the changed text and the contradicted text are located in the same area in the document making the in-text contradiction clear.

perturbation.original_text: At the request of the Discloser, Recipient will promptly return to the Discloser or at Discloser’s instructions destroy any written or electronic Confidential Information and all physical media on which Confidential Information was received from the other Recipient, including any copies thereof, with a letter confirming that the Confidential Information has not been reproduced or copied or that all copies have been returned and that none of the Confidential Information was disclosed to anyone except in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: At the request of the Discloser, Recipient will make reasonable efforts to return to the Discloser or at Discloser’s instructions destroy any written or electronic Confidential Information and all physical media on which Confidential Information was received from the other Recipient, including any copies thereof. A letter confirming the return or destruction is optional.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the Recipient's obligation to return or destroy Confidential Information by replacing 'promptly return' with 'make reasonable efforts to return' and making the confirmation letter optional. This contradicts the original text's clear requirement for prompt action and verification, creating uncertainty about whether the Discloser can truly ensure the confidentiality of their information is maintained after the relationship ends. This is an in-text contradiction because another section of the agreement states the Recipient shall take reasonable measures. What is considered reasonable at the end of the Agreement?
perturbation.contradicted_text: At the request of the Discloser, Recipient will make reasonable efforts to return to the Discloser or at Discloser’s instructions destroy any written or electronic Confidential Information and all physical media on which Confidential Information was received from the other Recipient, including any copies thereof. A letter confirming the return or destruction is optional. The Recipient and its Representatives shall not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions, in whole or in part, of such written or electronic material, except as may be needed to comply with applicable law and, provided that Recipient may retain one copy of Confidential Information in its confidential files for use only in the event a dispute arises between the Parties hereunder and only in connection with that dispute.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the recipient's obligation to return confidential information by changing "promptly return" to "make reasonable efforts to return" and making the confirmation letter optional. The contradicted text also uses "make reasonable efforts" but implies a stronger standard by adding that the Recipient and its Representatives shall not retain copies and limits the retention of copies. The changed text's weakening of the return obligation combined with the optional letter creates a contradiction with the more stringent requirements in the contradicted text. The change leads to ambiguity as to what constitutes 'reasonable efforts' when the contradicted text imposes tighter restrictions on the Recipient's ability to retain information. This creates an in-text contradiction.
